title: First Day at a Remote Job
body: Starting a new remote position can feel overwhelming. On your first day, you'll typically receive access to company tools like Slack, Jira, and Google Workspace. Your manager will schedule an onboarding call to introduce you to the team and explain your initial tasks. Don't worry if everything seems confusing at first — it takes about two weeks to feel comfortable with the workflow. The most important thing is to ask questions whenever you're unsure about something. Remote work requires strong asynchronous communication skills, so make sure to document your progress clearly.

questions.q: How long does it typically take to feel comfortable?
What skill is important for remote work?
What should you do when unsure about something?

title: Writing Effective Support Tickets
body: A well-written support ticket saves time for both the customer and the support agent. Start with a clear, specific subject line that summarizes the issue. In the description, include steps to reproduce the problem, expected behavior, and actual behavior. Attach screenshots if possible. Always mention your operating system, browser version, and any error messages you received. Avoid vague descriptions like 'it doesn't work' — instead, be specific: 'The Save button returns a 500 error when I upload a file larger than 10MB.' This helps the support team diagnose and resolve the issue faster.

questions.q: What makes a good subject line?
Why should you avoid 'it doesn't work'?

title: The Art of the Follow-Up Email
body: Following up on unanswered emails is a delicate art. Wait at least 48 hours before sending a follow-up — people are busy and may need time to respond. Your follow-up should be brief and polite. Start by referencing your previous email: 'I wanted to follow up on my email from Monday regarding the Q3 budget review.' Then restate your request clearly and concisely. If you have a deadline, mention it: 'I would appreciate your feedback by Thursday so we can include it in our presentation.' End with a professional closing. Never express frustration or impatience, even if you've followed up multiple times. The goal is to be persistent without being annoying.

questions.q: How long should you wait before following up?
What should you avoid in follow-up emails?
What is the goal of a follow-up?
options: To be persistent without being annoying

title: Understanding SaaS Metrics
body: SaaS companies track several key metrics to measure business health. Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR) is the predictable income from subscriptions each month. Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) measures how much it costs to acquire a new customer. Churn rate indicates the percentage of customers who cancel their subscriptions. Lifetime Value (LTV) estimates the total revenue a customer will generate over their entire relationship with the company. A healthy SaaS business typically has an LTV to CAC ratio of 3:1 or higher. Net Revenue Retention (NRR) measures revenue growth from existing customers, including upsells and expansions minus churn.

questions.q: What does MRR stand for?
What is a healthy LTV to CAC ratio?

title: Dealing with Difficult Customers
body: Handling difficult customers is an essential skill in customer support. The first step is to remain calm and empathetic. Acknowledge the customer's frustration: 'I understand how frustrating this must be.' Listen actively without interrupting. Once you understand the issue, take ownership: 'I'll personally make sure this gets resolved.' Avoid blaming other departments or making excuses. If you can't resolve the issue immediately, provide a clear timeline: 'I'll have an update for you within 24 hours.' Always follow through on your promises. Remember, a customer who had a problem resolved effectively often becomes more loyal than one who never had a problem.

questions.q: What is the first step when handling difficult customers?
What can make a customer more loyal?

title: Agile Methodology Basics
body: Agile is a project management methodology that emphasizes iterative development and continuous feedback. Instead of planning the entire project upfront, Agile teams work in short cycles called sprints, typically lasting 1-4 weeks. Each sprint delivers a working increment of the product. Daily standup meetings keep the team aligned — each member shares what they did yesterday, what they'll do today, and any blockers they face. At the end of each sprint, the team conducts a sprint review to demonstrate completed work and a retrospective to discuss improvements. Popular Agile frameworks include Scrum, Kanban, and Extreme Programming (XP).

questions.q: How long does a typical sprint last?
What happens in a daily standup?

title: Cross-Cultural Communication in Remote Teams
body: Working in international remote teams requires cultural awareness and communication skills. Different cultures have varying expectations about directness, hierarchy, and feedback. For example, some cultures prefer direct feedback ('This design doesn't work'), while others favor indirect communication ('Perhaps we could explore some alternatives'). Time zones add another layer of complexity — be mindful of scheduling meetings that work for all participants. Use clear, simple language and avoid idioms that may not translate well. Document decisions in writing to ensure everyone has access to the same information. When in doubt, ask for clarification rather than making assumptions about what a colleague means.

questions.q: Why should you avoid idioms in international teams?
What should you do when in doubt?

title: Effective Code Review Practices
body: Code review is a critical practice for maintaining code quality. When reviewing code, focus on logic errors, security vulnerabilities, and adherence to coding standards rather than style preferences. Provide constructive feedback: instead of 'This is wrong,' try 'Consider using a Map here for better performance — the current approach has O(n) lookup time.' Be specific about what needs to change and why. If the code is good, say so — positive feedback is just as important as constructive criticism. Review in small batches to avoid fatigue and ensure thoroughness. Remember that code review is a learning opportunity for both the reviewer and the author.

questions.q: What should code review focus on?
Why should you review in small batches?

title: Time Management for Remote Workers
body: Time management is crucial for remote workers who don't have the structure of an office environment. Start by establishing a consistent routine — wake up at the same time, get dressed, and create a dedicated workspace. Use time-blocking to schedule your day: allocate specific hours for focused work, meetings, and breaks. The Pomodoro Technique — working for 25 minutes followed by a 5-minute break — can help maintain focus. Set clear boundaries between work and personal time to avoid burnout. At the end of each day, plan tomorrow's priorities so you can start productively.

questions.q: What is the Pomodoro Technique?
Why are boundaries important for remote workers?

title: Negotiating Your Salary
body: Salary negotiation is a skill that can significantly impact your career earnings. Before negotiating, research market rates for your position using sites like Glassdoor, Levels.fyi, and LinkedIn Salary. Prepare a list of your accomplishments and the value you bring to the company. When discussing compensation, use a range rather than a single number, and anchor high. For example: 'Based on my research and experience, I'm looking for a range of $90,000 to $110,000.' Be prepared to discuss benefits, equity, and other non-salary compensation. If the offer is below your expectations, don't reject it immediately — ask for time to consider and come back with a counter-offer. Always maintain a professional and collaborative tone.

questions.q: What should you do before negotiating?
How should you present your salary expectations?
